title: Squeezed Phonon Lasing via Floquet-Controlled Solid-State Defects
authors: Hugo Molinares, Gianluca Rastelli, Victor Montenegro, Vitalie Eremeev
abstract: We propose a general Floquet-engineered scheme for phonon lasing that enables a continuous transition from conventional lasing to phase-locked squeezed phonon lasing. Focusing on a solid-state platform based on color centers embedded in a circular hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) membrane, we demonstrate that a mechanical oscillator coupled to principal and ancilla spins, and controlled via effective Floquet driving simultaneously exhibits squeezed-state amplification and cooling dynamics, leading to the emergence of a stable squeezed phonon laser. We analyse the steady-state properties of the system, including the lasing threshold, mechanical occupation, emission spectrum and second-order correlations. Furthermore, we show that Floquet engineering can realize phase-locked lasing while enabling controlled quadrature squeezing, thereby providing a simple yet effective route toward squeezed lasing in quantum mechanical systems. Our results offer new insights into the generation of squeezed phonon lasers in solid-state platforms, with potential applications in quantum metrology.
summary: This paper presents a theoretical framework for achieving squeezed phonon lasing in solid-state defects within hBN membranes using Floquet engineering. By carefully controlling the system's driving fields and coupling mechanisms, the authors demonstrate a pathway to stabilize the phase and generate robust, non-classical mechanical states. This offers a promising route for quantum metrology applications.
main_problem: The paper proposes a general Floquet-engineered scheme to achieve a continuous transition from conventional phonon lasing to phase-locked squeezed phonon lasing.
main_result: The scheme successfully demonstrates the realization of stable, steady-state squeezed phonon lasing in a solid-state platform by controlling the system's phase and quadrature fluctuations.
method: The analysis relies on deriving effective Hamiltonians using Floquet theory and the Magnus expansion, followed by solving the resulting Master Equation to characterize steady-state dynamics.
model_or_system: The physical system is a solid-state platform using color centers embedded in a hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) membrane, coupling a mechanical oscillator (phonons) to two principal and ancillary spins. The dynamics are governed by a complex Hamiltonian incorporating time-periodic driving terms.
key_observables: Lasing threshold, mechanical occupation ($\langle\hat{b}^\dagger\hat{b}angle_\infty$), second-order correlation function ($g^{(2)}(0)$), and the variance of generalized quadrature operators ($\langle(\Delta \hat{X}_	heta)^2angle$).
important_parameters:
Resonance conditions ($\Omega = \sum \Delta_j + \omega_m$), local driving frequencies ($
u$), and coupling strengths ($\lambda_1, J, \epsilon$).
main_assumptions: The analysis utilizes approximations such as the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA), adiabatic elimination of fast-decaying ancilla spins, and specific parameter hierarchies to simplify the effective Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of $g^{(2)}(0)$ and quadrature variance, showing convergence to values characteristic of lasing and squeezing, respectively. Wigner functions are used to visualize the transition to squeezed states.
paper_structure: The paper builds from a general theoretical proposal (Section II) to the specific application on the hBN platform (Section III), detailing the derivation of effective Hamiltonians for both squeezed amplification and phase-locked regimes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it combines several advanced topics: Floquet engineering for quantum control, solid-state quantum emitters, and the generation of non-classical states (squeezing) in mechanical oscillators, which are core areas of AMO physics and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The basic element of our theoretical proposal is a mechanical oscillator coupled to a spin, which interacts with a second spin via a driven spin-spin coupling, see Eq. (1). ωm is the mechanical oscillator frequency whereas ∆1, ∆2 are the energy splitting of the two spins. A local periodic drive is applied to the second spin at frequency ν whereas the spin-spin interaction is modulated at frequency Ω. (b) Numerical solutions of Eq. (2): Steady-state average phonon number ⟨ˆb†ˆb⟩∞as a function of ν/ωm, for the resonance condition Ω= ∆1 + ∆2 + ωm. Inset plot: Second-order correlation function. (c) Wigner function W(x, p) for selected values of ν/ωm with ε = ν/2. Top panel:...

FIG. 2. Scheme for the proposed setup: A suspended circular hBN membrane with radius R acts as the mechanical resonator. A mag- netic field gradient enables spin-motion coupling with spins placed at r1(r′ 1) (yellow spheres), each interacting with a second spin placed at r2(r′ 2) (green spheres) via a driven spin-spin coupling. A local pe- riodic drive is applied to the second spins.

FIG. 3. Steady-state solution of ME in Eq. (11): (a) Average phonon number ⟨ˆb†ˆb⟩∞and (b) second-order correlation function as a func- tion of amplitude driving field J/ωm, for different χ values. (c) Wigner representation W(x, p) at J = 0.3. (d) Power spectrum S (ω) as a function of J/ωm. Frequency defined with respect to the MO fre- quency ωm for χ = 1. Other parameters (in units of ωm): λ1 = 0.04, ν = ν′ = 2, Γj = 0.03, γm = 10−4, γ′ m = 10γm, ¯ns j = 10−5, ¯nm = 10−3.

FIG. 4. Numerical solution of ME in Eq. (16): (a) Time evolution of excitations and second-order correlation function, g(2)(0). (b) Time evolution of quadrature fluctuation ⟨(∆ˆXπ/2)2⟩, i.e. θ = π/2. Inset plot: Wigner function W(x, p) at t →∞. The green dashed lines in- dicate the contour at 0.1 max[W(x, p)], while the white dashed lines correspond to the same contour for a coherent field with identical coherent amplitude for θ = π/2.
